student_message: Oh cool! Moving shapes around to change the rules sounds exactly like making a beautiful kaleidoscope. What if we put six triangles together to build a hexagon, can it have both reflection and rotational symmetry at the same time?
final_message:
I love your kaleidoscope idea! You are completely right. A hexagon made of six triangles can absolutely have both kinds of symmetry at the same time! 🌟 

By carefully arranging shapes like your triangles, we can deliberately create beautiful designs that have reflection symmetry, rotational symmetry, or both. If you draw a straight line right down the middle of your hexagon, the two halves will match perfectly like a mirror, giving it reflection symmetry. And if you spin it just a little bit, which is a $$1/6$$ turn, every triangle moves into the next one's spot and it looks exactly the same, giving it rotational symmetry! 

You just created a perfect symmetrical design. If you wanted to color your hexagon, how would you paint the triangles so it keeps both types of symmetry?

orchestrator_intent.goal: Explain how a hexagon has both reflection and rotational symmetry.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: Student is happy, focused, and asked a great conceptual question that requires a conversational explanation.

current_section_content: You can deliberately create designs that have rotational symmetry, reflection symmetry, or both by carefully arranging or colouring shapes. A design has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after a fractional turn, and it has reflection symmetry if a mirror line splits it into two matching halves. The same set of shapes — such as squares and equilateral triangles with equal side lengths — can be arranged in different ways to produce designs with different symmetry properties. Designs can be sorted into three groups: only rotational symmetry, only reflection symmetry, or both.
session_summary: Zara covered reflectional and rotational symmetry in shapes, letters ('N', 'S'), and digital numbers. She understands these concepts exceptionally well, accurately differentiating between folding and spinning symmetry. Zara exhibited no struggles. Her learning state remains highly engaged and progressive; having mastered basic 2D symmetry, she readily applies concepts to new contexts and proactively asks advanced edge-case questions, such as whether a 6 rotating into a 9 counts as rotational symmetry.
